topic: A person finds out their biography was written without their knowledge
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp paper felt alien beneath his fingertips. Elias traced the elegant script, the carefully curated milestones that detailed his life—a life he barely recognized. A low hum of discontent vibrated in his chest, a heavy, leaden feeling that made his shoulders droop further. He reread the section about his early struggles, the phrasing too polished, too perfect. "It’s all just… a performance," he muttered, the sound barely escaping his throat. He crumpled the page slowly, the movement deliberate and heavy. The silence of his apartment amplified the thud of the paper hitting the worn rug. He stared at the window, watching the indifferent city lights blur, feeling a profound sense of being hollowed out, as if the very foundation of his existence had just been revealed as a fabrication.

[story 2]

When Clara finally laid the documents on the mahogany desk, the air in the room seemed to thin. She didn't look up immediately, instead focusing intently on a smudge on the antique wood. A tightness gripped her jaw, and she pressed her lips together until they were white. "This isn't right," she finally said, her voice flat, devoid of inflection. She picked up the biographical sketch, her fingers trembling slightly as she scanned the carefully chosen adjectives describing her achievements. A deep sigh escaped her, a sound heavy with resignation. She pushed the paper away, leaning back in her chair until the leather groaned in protest. Every muscle in her body felt coiled, ready to retract, unwilling to engage with the sudden, crushing weight of deception.

[story 3]

Mark sat at the cafe table, nursing a lukewarm coffee that had gone cold long ago. The folder lay open before him, the printed narrative of his life laid bare. He didn't touch it; instead, he picked at a loose thread on his sleeve, his gaze fixed on the condensation dripping down his glass. A slow, deep furrow settled between his brows. He took a deliberate, slow sip of the bitter liquid, letting the taste linger, focusing on the dull ache behind his eyes. *All of it*, he thought, the realization settling in like cold ash. The world outside the cafe window seemed too bright, too loud, and he felt utterly disconnected, as if he were watching a poorly rehearsed play from the wings.
